narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 32. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 and Year 3 ranking of 2 were both notably lower than the averages of around 5.7 and 5.5, respectively, placing you in the lower performance tiers compared to most students in the dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 47.1%, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a careful weighing of your academic history, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the single most influential factor, as it strongly pulled the model's assessment toward expecting you to pass by showcasing your college-level strength. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking was the next biggest driver, pushing the prediction toward failure because it signaled weaker performance in that critical later stage relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile played a smaller but still notable role in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Overall, while your GPA provided a significant boost, the combination of those lower decile rankings outweighed it, making your profile riskier than the typical applicant who tends to have stronger consistent school rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive background—many succeed despite similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar exam outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.7 average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplications, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also help reframe these metrics next time. With targeted improvements, your above-average GPA gives you a solid foundation to flip this prediction in future cycles.
